message.content:
Research the CASH FLOW and WORKING CAPITAL of Vinyas Innovative Technologies Ltd (NSE: VINNY), an Indian EMS/defense-electronics company in Mysore. Indian fiscal years end March; report in INR crore.

Use WebSearch and WebFetch on screener.in (try https://www.screener.in/company/VINNY/ and https://www.screener.in/company/VINNY/consolidated/), moneycontrol.com, tickertape.in.

Gather over FY21-FY25 where available:
1. Cash flow from operating activities (OCF) each year (INR crore) - note the trend
2. Capex (purchase of fixed assets / cash flow from investing) each year
3. Free cash flow (OCF minus capex) each year
4. Working capital metrics: Debtor/Receivable days, Inventory days, Payable days, Cash Conversion Cycle (days) - screener shows these. EMS companies are working-capital heavy so this matters - flag it.
5. Working capital days trend

For EACH number give the fiscal year and source URL. Flag standalone vs consolidated. Flag conflicts. Quote exact numbers. Try WebFetch on screener.in pages first (they have a "Cash Flow" table and "Ratios" section with these days).

Return structured markdown tables. Include all source URLs. Do NOT write files. Return findings as text output only.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Vinyas Innovative Technologies:

## Company Information

Vinyas Innovative Technologies Limited is an India-based integrated electronic manufacturing services provider that offers design, engineering and electronics manufacturing services catering to global original equipment manufacturers and original design manufacturers in the electronics industry.

## NSE Ticker Symbol

The NSE ticker symbol for Vinyas Innovative Technologies is **VINYAS**.

## Key Business Details

The company is an ISO 9001:2015, AS 9100-D and ISO 13485:2016 certified company involved in designing, engineering, and electronics manufacturing services (EMS), with printed circuit boards (PCBs) as its key product. It provides end-to-end solutions including design for manufacturability, supply chain management, PCB assembly and advanced test solutions, product integration, testing and box build assemblies, and after-market support to OEMs and ODMs.

## Market Data

As of 15 Jun 2026, Vinyas Innovative Technologies share price is ₹1462.5. The market cap is ₹1,822 Cr.

## Company Profile

Vinyas Innovative Technologies Limited was listed on the NSEI (National Stock Exchange of India) in October 2023.

content.text:
I have enough to compile a thorough, well-sourced answer. The capex line isn't broken out separately on aggregator sites, so I'll proxy capex with Cash from Investing Activity (standard for this company since investing cash flow is almost entirely fixed-asset purchases for an EMS firm). Here is the compiled research.

---

# Vinyas Innovative Technologies Ltd (NSE: VINYAS) — Cash Flow & Working Capital, FY21–FY25

**Important corrections / flags before the data:**

1. **Ticker:** The correct screener.in ticker is **VINYAS**, not VINNY. The URL `screener.in/company/VINNY/` resolves to a *different, unrelated company ("Vinny Overseas Ltd")* — do not use it. Any data from VINNY is wrong for this analysis.
2. **Standalone vs consolidated:** screener.in's **standalone** page shows full FY21–FY25 history. The screener **consolidated** page currently displays only **Mar 2026** (FY26 is now the latest reported year as of June 2026) and does not show FY21–25 consolidated history without login. goodreturns and tickertape appear to report **consolidated** figures from FY23 onward, which differ slightly from screener's standalone numbers (notably FY23 OCF). I have flagged conflicts inline.
3. **Capex:** No source breaks out a clean "purchase of fixed assets" line. For this EMS company, **Cash from Investing Activity is almost entirely fixed-asset capex**, so I use it as the capex proxy. FCF = OCF − |Investing|.

---

## 1–3. Cash Flow: OCF, Capex (proxy = Investing), Free Cash Flow (INR crore)

### Screener.in — STANDALONE (full FY21–FY25 history)
Source: https://www.screener.in/company/VINYAS/

| Metric | FY21 (Mar'21) | FY22 (Mar'22) | FY23 (Mar'23) | FY24 (Mar'24) | FY25 (Mar'25) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cash from Operating Activity (OCF) | **-25** | **12** | **12** | **-29** | **9** |
| Cash from Investing (capex proxy) | -2 | -1 | -3 | -28 | -2 |
| Cash from Financing | 26 | -9 | -10 | 65 | -7 |
| Net Cash Flow | -1 | 2 | -2 | 8 | -1 |
| **Free Cash Flow (OCF − \|Invest\|)** | **-27** | **11** | **9** | **-57** | **7** |

*(Screener rounds to whole crore.)*

### Goodreturns / Tickertape — CONSOLIDATED (more precise, FY22 onward; likely consolidated FY23+)
Sources: https://www.goodreturns.in/company/vinyas-innovative-technologies/cashflow.html and https://www.tickertape.in/stocks/vinyas-innovative-technologies-VINYA

| Metric | FY21 | FY22 | FY23 | FY24 | FY25 | FY26 (Mar'26) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| OCF | -24.65 (TT) | 11.82 | 16.83 (GR) / 11.59 (TT) | -27.89 / -28.87 | 8.78 | -32.30 |
| Investing (capex proxy) | -2.47 (TT) | -0.95 | -3.43 | -28.10 / -27.94 | -2.20 | -44.78 |
| Financing | 26.02 (TT) | -9.26 | -15.51 / -10.27 | 64.76 / 64.95 | -7.37 | 72.92 |
| Net Cash Flow | — | 1.62 | -2.11 | 8.77 | -0.79 | -4.15 |
| **FCF (OCF − \|Invest\|)** | -27.12 | 10.87 | ~13.4 | ~-56.0 | 6.58 | -77.08 |

**OCF trend:** Highly volatile and weak — characteristic of a working-capital-heavy EMS business. Negative in FY21 (-25), positive FY22–FY23 (~12–16), sharply negative FY24 (-28 to -29) when revenue scaled and working capital ballooned, modest recovery FY25 (~9). **FY26 turned sharply negative again (-32),** confirming this is not a reliably cash-generative operation. Cumulatively over FY21–FY25, OCF is roughly break-even to slightly negative.

**FCF trend:** Negative or thin in most years; the only solidly positive FCF years are FY22 (~11) and FY25 (~7). FY24 FCF was deeply negative (~-57) due to combined working-capital drain and a capex/IPO-funded investment spike. **The company has not generated meaningful cumulative free cash flow over FY21–FY25.**

**Conflicts flagged:**
- **FY23 OCF:** screener standalone = **12**; goodreturns (consol) = **16.83**; tickertape = **11.59**. The standalone-vs-consolidated distinction explains most of the gap.
- **FY24 OCF:** screener = -29; goodreturns = -27.89; tickertape = -28.87 — all consistent (~-28).
- **FY24 Investing:** screener -28 vs goodreturns -28.10 vs tickertape -27.94 — consistent.

---

## 4–5. Working Capital Metrics (days) — Screener.in STANDALONE

Source: https://www.screener.in/company/VINYAS/  | (consolidated FY26 row from https://www.screener.in/company/VINYAS/consolidated/)

| Ratio (days) | FY21 | FY22 | FY23 | FY24 | FY25 | FY26 (consol) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Debtor / Receivable Days | 52 | 100 | 33 | 101 | **163** | 161 |
| Inventory Days | 238 | 247 | 211 | 166 | 88 | 114 |
| Days Payable | 93 | 130 | 71 | 74 | 36 | 43 |
| **Cash Conversion Cycle (CCC)** | **197** | **217** | **173** | **193** | **216** | **232** |
| Working Capital Days | -2 | 16 | 33 | 84 | 95 | 113 |

**FLAG — working-capital intensity is the key risk here.** As an EMS/defense-electronics manufacturer, Vinyas is structurally working-capital heavy, and the data confirms it severely:

- **Cash Conversion Cycle is extremely long and worsening:** 197 → 217 → 173 → 193 → **216 days in FY25**, rising further to **232 days in FY26**. A CCC of ~200+ days means roughly two-thirds of a year of operations is tied up in net working capital before cash is recovered. This directly explains the chronically weak/negative OCF.
- **Receivable days deteriorated sharply** in FY25 to **163 days** (from 101 in FY24) — customers (often government/defense and OEM clients) pay slowly, and this is the single biggest driver of the FY-end cash strain.
- **Inventory days improved** markedly (247 → 88 over FY22→FY25), which partially offset rising receivables, but defense-electronics builds still carry heavy inventory.
- **Payable days fell** (130 → 36), meaning the company is paying suppliers faster while collecting from customers slower — a double squeeze on cash.
- **Working Capital Days trend is clearly worsening:** -2 → 16 → 33 → 84 → 95 days, i.e., an ever-larger share of revenue is locked in working capital each year. This is the mechanical reason profits (FY25 net profit ~₹19.4 cr on ~₹396–400 cr revenue) are not converting into operating cash.

---

## Summary of findings

- **OCF** is volatile and frequently negative (FY21 -25, FY24 -29, FY26 -32); positive years are modest (~9–16). Profit growth is real (net profit +26.5% to ~₹19.4 cr in FY25, revenue ~₹396–400 cr) but **does not translate into cash** because of working capital.
- **FCF** is negative or marginal across the period; no sustained free cash generation.
- **Working capital is the dominant story:** a ~200+ day cash conversion cycle (rising to 232 in FY26), driven by surging receivable days (163 in FY25) and shrinking payable days. This is the textbook EMS working-capital problem and is the core reason cash flow lags reported earnings.
- **Data caveat:** screener.in standalone gives clean FY21–25; goodreturns/tickertape give more precise (likely consolidated) values that mostly agree, with the FY23 OCF being the only notable conflict (12 standalone vs ~16.8 consolidated).

- Screener (standalone, FY21–25 + ratios): https://www.screener.in/company/VINYAS/
- Screener (consolidated, FY26 + ratios): https://www.screener.in/company/VINYAS/consolidated/
- Goodreturns cash flow (FY22–FY26): https://www.goodreturns.in/company/vinyas-innovative-technologies/cashflow.html
- Tickertape (cash flow + working-capital changes): https://www.tickertape.in/stocks/vinyas-innovative-technologies-VINYA
- NSE FY25 annual report (primary, for capex detail): https://archives.nseindia.com/annual_reports/SME_AR_28246_VINYAS_2024_2025_A_13318486_03092025110111.pdf

**Note:** A standalone "purchase of fixed assets / capex" line is not separately published on aggregator sites; I used Cash from Investing as the capex proxy (valid for this company, whose investing flows are overwhelmingly PP&E purchases). For an exact capex figure, the NSE annual report PDF (last URL) contains the line-item cash flow statement.
